feedback.text: There was recently a controversy in the US about how payment processors (Visa and Mastercard) was able to censor what people where allowed to pay for or not. This i believe is highly problematic as i do not think it is the responsibility of a payment processor to play the role of moral police of what i may or may not spend my own money on. It is the job of my own country or the EU to decide what i can buy legally and the payment processors job is exclusively to process my payment, they can track it if they must in, case it goes to something illegal. But it is not their job to decide what they deem right or wrong where my money goes. In a worst case scenario i could have said something about a political figure and afterwards i would have no access to my own money to pay for things like food or bills. Since Visa and Mastercard practically has a monopoly on digital payment processing, it is even more important that they are legally forced to be politically neutral. I personally fear the consequences if any sort of political activism can simply write letters to them and an entire industry that is NOT breaking the law is suddenly getting punished because around a thousand people send letters saying they should, as was the case in the recent controversy. It was a group known as "Collective Shout" that claimed responsibility for the censorship and claimed they send a little over 1000 letters, apparently 1000 letters was enough to force the hand of Visa. If people only need to write around 1000 letters to force the hand of the biggest payment processors to adhere to their political ideology, then that is an incredibly dangerous road ahead with drastically big consequences. Sincerely a concerned citizen.
